title: Expressions
text: Expressions An expression is a ProcScript statement that, after execution, resolves to a value. An expression cannot be executed by itself; it is always used as part of an assignment, condition, or argument, or in a string substitution. In Uniface, every expression has a string representation. As a consequence, an expression can always be assigned to or substituted in a string. Uniface performs implicit data type conversion if the expression is of a data type other then String. CAUTION: When testing conditions, you should not rely on implicit type conversion to use strings as if they were Booleans. You should always explicitly state what you are testing for. For example, use if (var1 = "true") , not if (var1) . Example: Expressions in Conditions, Assignments, and Arguments The following ProcScript examples show how expressions can be used: In an assignment: myVar = "My expression" Copy In a condition: if (myVar = "My expression") var1 = var2 if ($1 = "My expression") $2 = $1 Copy As an argument of a ProcScript Statement: putmess "My expression" Copy As an argument of a ProcScript Function: $1 = $lowercase("My expression") Copy ProcScript Function $time used as an expression in an assignment: $1 = $time Copy ProcScript Function $time used as an expression in string substitution: putmess "The current time is %%$time%%%" Copy Related Links $expression Return the result of evaluating a nonconditional expression. Operators Operators perform arithmetic, relational, logical, string manipulation, struct member de-reference, operation de-reference, and field indirection operations in ProcScript instructions.
